title: How to Get Rid of Scratches on Glasses: What Works and What Doesn't
description: Scratched glasses? Most DIY fixes damage coatings further. Learn which methods to avoid, when to replace lenses, and how to prevent future scratches.
tldr: There is no safe way to remove scratches from coated prescription lenses. DIY methods (toothpaste, baking soda, wax) strip protective coatings and make things worse. Minor edge scratches can be tolerated. Central scratches that affect vision mean it's time for new lenses ($80-$250 for single vision). Prevention through proper care is the only real solution.

definition.text: The attempted repair of physical damage to eyeglass lens surfaces. On modern coated lenses, true scratch removal is not possible without destroying the lens coatings. The practical solution is lens replacement or, for minor scratches, acceptance and improved care habits.

sections.h2: The Truth About Removing Scratches from Glasses
sections.content:
If you've searched for this, you've probably already seen articles recommending toothpaste, baking soda, or car wax. Here's what those articles don't mention: those methods damage your lenses further.

Modern prescription lenses aren't plain glass or plastic. They have multiple microscopic coating layers — anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, UV protection, possibly blue light filtering. These coatings are bonded to the lens surface and can't be selectively repaired.

A scratch either sits in the coating layer or cuts through to the lens material. Any abrasive compound (toothpaste, baking soda, polish) strips the surrounding coating, creating uneven areas that cause worse visual distortion than the original scratch.

The optical industry's honest position: scratches on coated lenses cannot be repaired. They can only be prevented or addressed with new lenses.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: DIY Methods That Actually Cause Damage
sections.content:
• Toothpaste: Contains calcium carbonate or silica abrasives. Strips anti-reflective coating, leaving cloudy patches that scatter light worse than the scratch.
  • Baking soda paste: More aggressively abrasive than toothpaste. Same result, faster damage.
  • Vehicle wax (Turtle Wax, etc.): Fills the scratch temporarily with wax that smears within hours and attracts dust. Creates a film that degrades anti-reflective properties.
  • Furniture polish or Pledge: Leaves an oily residue that interacts poorly with lens coatings. May cause cloudiness that's difficult to remove.
  • Armour Etch (glass etching cream): Contains hydrofluoric acid. Extremely dangerous to handle and destroys lens coatings immediately.
  • Rubbing alcohol: Won't remove scratches but can damage hydrophobic coatings with repeated use.

The one exception: truly uncoated glass lenses (rare today) can sometimes be polished by a professional using cerium oxide compound. But this doesn't apply to modern prescription lenses — virtually all are plastic/polycarbonate with coatings.

sections.h2: When to Replace Scratched Lenses vs. Live with Them
sections.content:
Not every scratch needs immediate action. Evaluate based on location and severity:

Replace your lenses if:

  • Scratches are in your central field of vision (the area you look through most)
  • You notice glare from oncoming headlights at night
  • Multiple scratches create an overall hazy appearance
  • Lens coatings are peeling, flaking, or crazing (web-like cracks)

You can live with scratches if:

  • They're near the lens edge, outside your typical viewing area
  • They're hairline scratches only visible when you inspect the lens closely
  • They don't cause glare or visual interference during daily activities

Replacing lenses in your existing frames is typically $80-$250 for single vision or $200-$600 for progressives. Some optical stores can replace single vision lenses same-day, so you won't be without your glasses for long.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

sections.h2: How to Prevent Scratches Going Forward
sections.content:
Once you replace scratched lenses (or if you want to protect new ones), these habits make a real difference:

  • Always rinse before wiping. This is the single most important habit. Dust particles on a dry lens act like sandpaper when you wipe. Running water removes them safely.
  • Use microfiber cloths exclusively. Paper towels, tissues, napkins, and clothing fabrics all contain materials that scratch. Microfiber is specifically designed for optical surfaces.
  • Store in a hard case. Not in your pocket, not face-down on a table, not loose in a bag or purse. A rigid case prevents contact with keys, coins, and other abrasive objects.
  • Clean your microfiber cloth regularly. A dirty microfiber holds trapped grit that transfers to your lenses. Wash weekly without fabric softener.
  • Choose scratch-resistant coating. Not scratch-proof (nothing is), but it adds a harder surface layer that resists minor abrasion. Usually included in mid-range lens packages.

Ask your optician about coating warranties when purchasing new lenses. Some lens manufacturers offer a one-time replacement if coatings scratch within the first year. This protection is one advantage of buying from an established optical store with manufacturer relationships.

When scratches are bad enough to replace, you have two paths:

Replace lenses only (keep your frames): The more economical option. Your optician orders new lenses custom-cut for your existing frame. Works as long as the frame is in good structural condition. Most optical stores offer this service.

Get a complete new pair: Makes sense if your frames are also worn, damaged, or if your prescription has changed (meaning you need new lenses anyway). Check whether your insurance benefits have reset — you might be due for a fully covered pair.

Either way, consider upgrading your coating package. If your previous lenses scratched easily, moving up to a premium scratch-resistant or premium anti-reflective package (which usually includes better scratch resistance) extends the life of the investment. Your optician can recommend the right coating level for your lifestyle.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Can scratches be buffed out of prescription glasses?
faqs.a: No. Modern prescription lenses have thin coatings that can't be selectively repaired. Buffing or polishing removes the coatings entirely, creating worse optical problems than the original scratch. The only fix is lens replacement.

No, and it makes things worse. Toothpaste contains abrasives that strip anti-reflective and other coatings from your lenses. The result is uneven, cloudy patches that interfere with vision more than the scratch did.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to replace scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Replacing lenses in your existing frame costs $80-$250 for single vision and $200-$600 for progressive lenses, depending on material and coatings. This is typically covered by insurance if your benefits have renewed.

Yes. Scratch-resistant coating doesn't make lenses scratch-proof, but it significantly extends their lifespan. The coating adds a harder surface layer that resists everyday abrasion from dust and minor contact. Most mid-range lens packages include it standard.

faqs.q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.

faqs.q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
faqs.a: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.
